competence_markers.description: The child understands commitment as keeping your word regardless of changing feelings
The child can describe what marriage looks like from the partnerships they observe
The child demonstrates reliability in small commitments — observed over time
The child understands family as a team and contributes to it

narration_prompts.prompt: What does it mean to keep a promise?
narration_prompts.detail: Can you think of a time you kept a promise even when it was hard?

narration_prompts.prompt: Why is marriage called the biggest promise?
narration_prompts.detail: What makes it hard to keep?

narration_prompts.prompt: How is a family like a team?
narration_prompts.detail: What is your role on your family team?

narration_prompts.prompt: What happens when someone breaks a promise?

cross_connections.description: Promise-keeping is IC1's foundational capacity applied to relationships.
Loyalty and reliability in friendship are practice for loyalty and reliability in marriage.
Love as action (not just feeling) connects directly to commitment as practice (not just promise).
The family team concept developed here connects to 4.4.7's deeper treatment of family formation.

content.why_this_matters:
A promise is one of the most powerful things a person can make. When you make a promise, you are giving your word — telling another person that they can count on you, that you will do what you said you would do, even when it is hard, even when you do not feel like it, even when something better comes along.

Marriage is the biggest promise most people ever make — a promise to stay with one person, to work together, to love each other through good times and hard times, for the rest of their lives. You are years away from making that promise. But you can start learning what it means to keep your word right now, because a person who cannot keep small promises will not be able to keep big ones.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **The concept of commitment** — keeping your word even when it is hard
2. **Stories of enduring marriages** — models of what partnership through difficulty looks like
3. **Family as a team** — the concept that the people in your home are building something together
4. **The cost of broken promises** — understanding that broken commitments damage trust
5. **Practice through small commitments** — building reliability through daily follow-through
content.core_content:
### Part 1: What a Promise Means

A promise is simple to say and hard to keep. When you promise something — "I will be there," "I will help you," "I will finish this" — you are putting your word on the line. Your word is your reputation. A person whose word means something is trusted. A person whose word means nothing is alone.

Every time you keep a promise, you build something invisible but real: trust. The people around you learn that they can count on you. That trust is the most valuable thing you will ever build in any relationship — more valuable than gifts, more valuable than fun, more valuable than anything except love itself.

Every time you break a promise, you damage that trust. Not permanently — trust can be rebuilt — but it takes longer to rebuild than it took to break. One broken promise can undo months of reliability. This is why keeping your word matters so much, even in small things.

### Part 2: Marriage — The Biggest Promise

Marriage is a promise between two people to build a life together. It is the most important promise most people will ever make, and it is the hardest to keep — not because married people stop loving each other, but because life is long and hard and full of moments when keeping the promise requires everything you have.

When you see married adults around you, what you are seeing is a promise in action — two people who decided to work together, to stay together through difficulty, to forgive each other's failures, and to keep building even when building is hard.

Some marriages struggle. Some marriages end. When that happens, it is sad — because a promise has been broken, and broken promises always hurt. But understanding that marriage requires work, commitment, and persistence helps you prepare to make it well someday.

### Part 3: Family as a Team

The family you live in is a team. Like any team, everyone has a role. Parents lead and provide. Children contribute and learn. Everyone helps. Everyone matters. The whole is more than the parts.

A good team does not fall apart when things get hard. When a storm comes, when someone gets sick, when money is tight, when someone makes a mistake — a good team draws closer, not further apart. The people in your family are the people who will be there when everyone else has gone home. Treating them well — even when you are tired, frustrated, or angry — is practice for every commitment you will ever make.

### Part 4: Practice Keeping Your Word

You can build the commitment muscle right now, in small ways:
- If you say you will do something, do it. Even if you do not feel like it. Even if something more fun comes up.
- If you start a project, finish it. Quitting when things get hard trains you to quit. Finishing when things get hard trains you to persist.
- If you make a mistake and need to apologize, follow through on the commitment to do better. An apology without changed behavior is not a commitment — it is just words.
- If someone is counting on you, be there. Show up. Every time.

These are small things. But small things, done consistently, build the kind of person who can keep the biggest promises life will ask of you.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Promise-Keeping
**Developing well:** The child follows through on stated commitments. They demonstrate growing reliability.
**Developing slowly:** The child makes promises casually and breaks them frequently without apparent concern.

### 2. Marriage Understanding
**Developing well:** The child can describe marriage as a long-term commitment that requires work, not just feelings.
**Developing slowly:** The child understands marriage only through fairy-tale narratives (love at first sight, happily ever after without effort).

### 3. Family Contribution
**Developing well:** The child actively contributes to the family team — chores, helping, cooperation.
**Developing slowly:** The child sees the family as a service provider rather than a team they contribute to.
